I have found the [python issue #5117](http://bugs.python.org/issue5117) that seems explaining the difference in the ``os.path.relpath`` execution with Python 2.6.9, 2.7.9, 3.4.2 and 3.5.2.
A more direct approach could be to *practically* seen the problem. You can do it running the script that is shown below (are added also the results that I have on my laptop).

I have not found an *official* reply to your doubt; but I'm able to provide you a short snippet that highlights the issue.

Script
```bash
for version in 2.6.9 2.7.9 3.4.2 3.5.2; do
    pyenv global $version
    python -c "import os; import sys; print(sys.version); print(os.path.relpath('/dir1/../dir2/file.json', '/'))"
    echo "=========="
done
```

My Results
```bash
2.6.9 (unknown, Jul  1 2016, 10:58:34) 
[GCC 4.2.1 Compatible Apple LLVM 7.3.0 (clang-703.0.31)]
../dir2/file.json
==========
2.7.9 (default, Jul  1 2016, 10:52:19) 
[GCC 4.2.1 Compatible Apple LLVM 7.3.0 (clang-703.0.31)]
dir2/file.json
==========
3.4.2 (default, Jul  1 2016, 10:56:36) 
[GCC 4.2.1 Compatible Apple LLVM 7.3.0 (clang-703.0.31)]
dir2/file.json
==========
3.5.2 (default, Jul  3 2016, 11:59:24) 
[GCC 4.2.1 Compatible Apple LLVM 7.3.0 (clang-703.0.31)]
dir2/file.json
```
**NOTE**: I'm using pyenv to quickly switch between python version. Each command execution shows the used version and the result of the ``os.path.relpath('/dir1/../dir2/file.json', '/')`` command.

This is just fixing a bug from 5 years ago to make the regex actually do what it was originally intended to do.  One method of escaping the hyphen is to make it the last character in a set and that's what was done first.  An edit was made to put the hyphen in the middle, but wasn't properly escaped so `+-/` was expanded to `+,-./` and was not what was intended.  This is a very simple patch to stop matching `,` as was originally intended.

Asking @dwt to write an extensive comment on a regex from 5 years ago that they didn't compose seems overly burdensome.  I think we can all agree that the `,` doesn't belong in the regex and we can confidently commit just that fix without re-examining everything else in the regex.

The `rng` attribute for `RealGalaxy` is used in (up to) three places: to optionally pick a random index from the catalog, to pad the input galaxy image when creating the `InterpolatedImage`, and to initialize the returned object's `noise`(`covspec`) attribute.  For the first two of these, I'd argue that once the index and noise-padded images are created, the `rng` isn't needed any more for equality testing, hashing, or eval(repr())-ing.  For the third case, there may be an argument that two `RealGalaxy`s that are the same except for the `rng` of their noise attributes should be treated as unequal, but I'd note that this isn't how we test for equality / hash / eval(repr()) any other `GSObject`s, which can also, in principle, have `noise` attributes.  

So I think my proposal would be to not add `rng` (or `_rng`) as an attribute to either `RealGalaxy` or `ChromaticRealGalaxy`, not include `noise` (or `covspec`) in the equality testing or hashing, and have eval(repr()) create an object with the same `noise` or `covspec` variance/covariance, but with different `rng`s from the original.  We'd probably want to add some documentation too clarifying that two `RG`s or `CRG`s (really all `GSObject`s and `ChromaticObject`s) can compare equal despite different noise/covspec attribute rngs.

Also, there isn't a single "right" way to name a test, but what we've generally done is this: `test_[method_name]_[aspect_under_test]`.  See, for example, the tests here: https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/wikitables/tests/data/semparse/knowledge_graphs/table_knowledge_graph_test.py.  This makes it a lot easier to know what the point of the test is, and if you end up breaking the test later, you can see in the name what functionality you've broken.

There are also times when there isn't a clear "aspect" that's under test, other than the standard flow through the method with a typical expected input.  In those cases, I typically just name it `test_[method_name]`.  I'd say this test falls under that category, so I'd name this `test_get_neighbor_indices`.

Such a subclass of MessageBase is not needed. Actually `FieldValidator` and its subclasses (`MessageBase` is one of them) are used for validation against schemas. Now `ActionResult` is not used in any schemas since `Reply.result` is defined as `AnyValueField`.

Moreover, `Reply.result` for an action should be just a dictionary with the specific fields, it should not be a simple class, because it must be transferred via network as a dictionary. For example, see `Node.handle_get_txn_req` - just a dictionary is used as `Reply.result`. (If you saw at `MessageBase` subclasses, please note that `MessageBase` is a subclass of `Mapping`, so `MessageBase` provides conversion to a dictionary. But in our case we should use just a dictionary because we need not validation facilities for the reply content.)

Good eye. The answer is "sort of." If you look through the library you'll find lots of places where parse errors can occur, raising exceptions. This is pretty standard in OOP. The problem is that this makes the code harder to work with. For example, the user/client may provide an extra unneeded attribute value, or instead omit one, when making a client call. This mistake may not trigger an exception until way down the processing stack when reading or writing an entire request/response structure. This is frustrating to the user because they get an error related to library internals that they don't understand, and its frustrating for developers because the source of the error is far away from where the error is actually detected, making it hard to find and fix.

There are a couple of solutions to this. The obvious one is to just put in better error handling. The problem is this would pretty much have to happen everywhere and it would even further bloat and complicate the code. An alternative (which is what I explored for a time) was to rethink what is actually a parse error. The idea here is that if a request/response message can be parsed successfully (i.e., the fields present are formatted properly and there are no partial fields that can't get read), let it get parsed. If the payload is missing certain fields, let the user/client know by a more organic, less intrusive mechanism than just erroring out. I started playing around with this with the `GetAttributeListResponsePayload`, to see if I liked how it felt/worked and if it caused any problems with active deployments. I don't have enough "data" yet to determine if it's a viable way forward, so I'm not sure if I'll stick with it.

For now, with the MAC payloads, I'm fine with you putting in those read/write exceptions for missing required fields. If need be, I'll patch in the right exception(s) needed for `GetAttributeList` and make everything consistent again.

The merkle tree is composed of the locks hashes:
> I now need to create locked_encoded from those 3 values.

`locked_encoded` is a just the three values concatenated `expiration_block || amount || hashlock`. After it is concatenated together we take the hash of it:

https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/f32a14713aafea62a6f5b7616c8799ed8f8f6a32/raiden/smart_contracts/NettingChannelLibrary.sol#L439

IOW, the merkle tree is composed of the hash of the encoded locks, and that is equivalente to `keccak256(expiration_block, amount, hashlock);`

> keccak256(...) returns (bytes32): compute the Ethereum-SHA-3 (Keccak-256) hash of the (tightly packed) arguments

https://solidity.readthedocs.io/en/develop/miscellaneous.html#global-variables

exactly. If `address_encoder()` returns checksummed address, then the comparison will be true for values returned from `web3`.

It's also possible to compare checksummed/non-checksummed addrs using `is_same_address()`, but since the event parameters also include hashes, it would be a little more complicated.

`EnqueuePlaceableTask` basically makes the raylet take ownership of the task, by putting it either into the `waiting` or `ready` queue. In contrast, `QueuePlaceableTasks` will re-enqueue the task as `placeable`, so that we can make another decision about its placement. We should remember that this code executes when `ForwardTask` fails, which likely happens because the remote raylet died. In such a case, I would expect that our cluster resources data structure is updated, removing the dead raylet from the resource map. When that happens, the policy will no longer "make the same decision".

In the test you are referencing, @robertnishihara, it might be hanging because  the cluster resource map is not updated to remove the dead raylet. We should double check that this mechanism is actually firing and the cluster resource map is updated. However, I am against automatically taking ownership of the task and bypassing the scheduling policy when we fail to forward it. Letting the scheduling policy decide what to do with that task should be strictly better.

No, we cannot write CubicSpline to a netCDF file, but there is an actual output field `snow_ice_temperature` that has a fixed shape that _can_ be written. This field is created by evaluating the spline at a fixed number of levels, which is decided during component instantiation.

For this particular component, using a CubicSpline makes the actual algorithm we implement to calculate ice temperatures and growth/melting much easier. We would otherwise need an array whose dimension varies in space (since you want to use the same dz for all columns), and I don't think I have the bandwidth to carefully think that out.

I agree there is a definite use case for variable shaped arrays, but I think we need a concrete component whose demands sympl will need to satisfy to take up further design towards this end. Until then, I think our energy is better spent elsewhere -- a lot of work still remains to make sympl/climt actually fully usable in terms of convenience.
